claim: Approximately 14% of US dog-owning households lost their dog at least once in a 5-year period, and 93% of those lost dogs were subsequently recovered.
stance_target: empirical baseline for dog loss and recovery frequency
scope_conditions: National US random-digit-dial telephone sample, Sept–Nov 2010, land-line households only, 5-year recall window
quoted_support: Fourteen percent of dogs (95% Confidence Interval (CI): 11–16%) and 15% (95% CI: 12–18%) of cats were lost in the past five years... Ninety three percent (95% CI: 86–97%) of dogs and 75% (95% CI: 64–85%) of cats were recovered.

claim: Neighborhood search is the dominant recovery method for lost dogs (49%), whereas lost cats predominantly return home on their own (59%).
stance_target: active neighborhood search as primary dog recovery strategy
scope_conditions: National US sample 2010, only pets that were successfully recovered
quoted_support: I found my pet by searching my neighborhood: 49% [of dogs]... My pet returned on its own: 20% [dogs] vs 59% [cats]
numbers: 49% of dogs recovered via neighborhood search

claim: ID tags were the primary recovery method for 15% of lost dogs, confirming identification as a meaningful factor in recovery probability.
stance_target: identification tags as causal factor in recovery
quoted_support: I was contacted because of a tag my pet was wearing/my pet's microchip: 15 [of 101 recovered dogs] (15%, 9–23%)
numbers: 15% ID tag recovery rate for dogs (CI 9–23%)
61% of lost dogs were wearing personalized ID tag at time of loss
only 1 of 54 recovered cats recovered via tag

claim: No basic pet or owner demographic variable (sex, neuter status, education, income, gender) independently predicted whether a dog or cat became lost.
stance_target: demographic predictors of dog loss event
scope_conditions: Multivariable logistic regression, US national sample, limited statistical power (n=110 lost dogs)
quoted_support: Our logistic regression analysis did not find that basic pet or owner demographics were associated with pets getting lost... This implies that more complex variables relating to pet environments and owner lifestyles will need to be studied.

claim: Neutered lost pets and those belonging to higher-educated owners were significantly more likely to be recovered, suggesting owner engagement and pet management practices drive reunion probability.
stance_target: owner-side factors as drivers of recovery probability
scope_conditions: US national sample 2010; small subsample of unfound pets limits power
quoted_support: We did find that lost neutered pets, lost pets belonging to respondents with more education, and lost cats belonging to respondents with higher income were more likely to be reunited with their owners.
numbers: p=0.03 for neuter status vs. recovery (dogs)
p=0.007 for neuter status vs. recovery (cats)
p=0.03 for education vs. recovery (both species)

claim: The majority of dog reunions occur outside the shelter system; only ~6% of successfully recovered dogs were found via animal control or shelter visit.
stance_target: non-shelter recovery as dominant dog reunion pathway
scope_conditions: US national 2010; n=101 successfully recovered dogs
quoted_support: I found my pet by visiting/contacting animal control: 6 [dogs] (6%, 2–12%)... There were 6,996 calls made of which only 4,029 were households.
numbers: 7% of dogs and 25% of cats were never reunited
